name: Yang Jian
description: Yang Jian, also known as Erlang Shen, is a prominent hero in Chinese mythology, often depicted as a powerful warrior and god. He is the nephew of the Jade Emperor, ruler of Heaven, born from a mortal woman and a god. Yang Jian possesses immense strength, martial prowess, and magical abilities, most notably his third eye located in his forehead, which grants him enhanced vision and the power to see through illusions and perceive truth. He is a skilled shapeshifter, capable of transforming into various animals and objects. Yang Jian is often portrayed as a righteous and independent figure, sometimes defying the authority of Heaven to uphold justice and protect mortals. He played a crucial role in numerous mythical battles, including the suppression of the Monkey King, Sun Wukong, during the latter's rebellion. Despite his divine lineage, Yang Jian often chooses to remain aloof from the celestial bureaucracy, preferring to wander the mortal realm and assist those in need. His unwavering loyalty, formidable power, and commitment to justice have cemented his place as one of the most beloved and respected heroes in Chinese folklore. He is a symbol of strength, integrity, and the ability to overcome even the most formidable challenges, embodying the ideal of a warrior-scholar.
subtitle: The Divine Warrior with the Third Eye
achievements: Protecting the mortal realm from celestial threats

keyMyths.title: Imprisonment of Yang Jian's Mother
keyMyths.description: Yang Jian's mother was imprisoned beneath a mountain for marrying a mortal. Yang Jian cleaved the mountain in two with his axe, freeing his mother and demonstrating his filial piety and immense strength.

keyMyths.title: The Battle Against Sun Wukong
keyMyths.description: Yang Jian played a pivotal role in capturing Sun Wukong during the Monkey King's rebellion against Heaven. He engaged Sun Wukong in a fierce battle of transformations and martial skill, ultimately leading to the Monkey King's subjugation.

keyMyths.title: The Third Eye
keyMyths.description: Yang Jian's third eye grants him incredible perception and the ability to see through illusions. It is a symbol of his divine heritage and his ability to discern truth from falsehood, making him a formidable opponent against deceptive enemies.

sources.author: Xu Zhonglin
sources.description: A major source for Yang Jian's origins and his role in the Shang-Zhou transition.
sources.text: A major source for Yang Jian's origins and his role in the Shang-Zhou transition.

sources.text: Features Yang Jian's battle against Sun Wukong and showcases his abilities.

associations.name: Double-Edged Sword
associations.significance: Represents his martial prowess and divine authority.

associations.name: Howling Celestial Dog
associations.significance: A loyal companion symbolizing protection and tracking abilities.

associations.name: Third Eye
associations.significance: Grants enhanced vision, truth-seeing, and mystical power.

associations.name: Nephew of the Jade Emperor
associations.significance: Highlights his divine lineage and complex relationship with Heaven.

associations.name: 72 Transformations
associations.significance: Represents adaptability, resourcefulness, and mastery over the natural world.

associations.name: Filial Piety
associations.significance: Motivates his actions, particularly his quest to rescue his mother.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A divine hero with immense strength who undertakes great labors.
A powerful warrior associated with divine authority and protection, often battling monstrous foes.
A divine figure known for his martial prowess, rebellious spirit, and ultimate triumph over chaos.

worshipPractices.name: Temples and Shrines
worshipPractices.description: Temples and shrines are dedicated to Yang Jian throughout China and in Chinese communities worldwide. Devotees offer prayers, incense, and food as offerings, seeking his blessings for protection, strength, and success. Specific rituals may vary regionally, but generally involve honoring his divine status and seeking his intervention in worldly affairs.

cultural.modernLegacy: Yang Jian remains a popular figure in modern Chinese culture, appearing in numerous films, television series, video games, and literature. He embodies strength, justice, and filial piety, resonating with audiences seeking heroic figures. His image is used in advertising and popular art, solidifying his place as a cultural icon.

extendedContent.title: Early Life
extendedContent.content: Yang Jian's birth is shrouded in miraculous circumstances. His mother, a mortal woman, conceived him after encountering a divine being. This union angered the Jade Emperor, ruler of Heaven, as it violated the laws forbidding relationships between gods and mortals. Yang Jian was born with a third eye in his forehead, a symbol of his divine heritage and extraordinary powers. His mother was imprisoned under a mountain for her transgression. This early injustice fueled Yang Jian's determination to rescue her and challenge the rigid authority of the heavenly court. His early life was marked by hardship and a yearning for justice, setting the stage for his future heroic deeds. He trained in martial arts and magic, honing his skills in preparation for the challenges ahead. This period instilled in him a deep sense of responsibility and a commitment to protecting the innocent from injustice. His devotion to his mother and his desire to right the wrongs committed against her became central to his character.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests
extendedContent.content: Yang Jian's most renowned quest is his effort to rescue his mother from imprisonment under Mount Tao. Displaying immense strength and determination, he battled celestial warriors and overcame numerous obstacles to reach her. Using his divine axe, he split the mountain, freeing his mother and defying the Jade Emperor's decree. This act solidified his reputation as a rebellious hero willing to challenge even the highest authority for the sake of justice and filial piety. Another significant quest involves his participation in suppressing the Monkey King, Sun Wukong. Despite Sun Wukong's formidable powers, Yang Jian proved to be one of the few capable of matching him in combat. Their epic battle showcased Yang Jian's martial prowess and strategic thinking. Although initially an opponent, Yang Jian later worked alongside Sun Wukong to defeat other powerful demons and maintain cosmic order, demonstrating his willingness to cooperate for the greater good.

extendedContent.title: Legacy
extendedContent.content: Yang Jian's legacy extends far beyond his individual feats of strength and courage. He represents a complex figure who embodies both divine power and human compassion. His willingness to challenge authority and fight for justice resonates with those who feel oppressed or marginalized. He is a symbol of filial piety, demonstrating the importance of family bonds and the lengths one should go to protect loved ones. In contemporary Chinese culture, Yang Jian continues to be a popular figure, appearing in numerous films, television shows, and video games. His image is often used to represent strength, justice, and unwavering determination. He serves as an inspiration to those who strive to overcome adversity and fight for what they believe in. His story continues to be retold and reinterpreted, ensuring that his legacy as a heroic figure endures for generations to come. Yang Jian's enduring appeal lies in his ability to embody both the divine and the human, making him a relatable and inspiring figure for people of all backgrounds.

shortDescription: Yang Jian, or Erlang Shen, is a powerful warrior-god in Chinese mythology. Nephew of the Jade Emperor, he's known for his third eye and martial prowess.
